elements.sent-2411-001: Natural policy gradient ($\NPG$) is a common policy optimization algorithm and can be viewed as mirror ascent in the space of probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-002: Recently,~\citet{vaswani2021general introduced a policy gradient method that corresponds to mirror ascent in the dual space of logits.}
elements.sent-2411-003: We refine this algorithm, removing its need for a normalization across actions and analyze the resulting method (referred to as $\Alg$).
elements.sent-2411-004: For tabular MDPs, we prove that $\Alg$ with a constant step-size matches the linear convergence of $\NPG$ and achieves a faster convergence than constant step-size (accelerated) softmax policy gradient.
elements.sent-2411-005: To handle large state-action spaces, we extend $\Alg$ to use a log-linear policy parameterization.
elements.sent-2411-006: Unlike that for $\NPG$, generalizing $\Alg$ to the linear function approximation (FA) setting does not require compatible function approximation.
elements.sent-2411-007: Unlike $\MDPO$, a practical generalization of $\NPG$, $\Alg$ with linear FA only requires solving convex softmax classification problems.
elements.sent-2411-008: We prove that $\Alg$ achieves linear convergence to the neighbourhood of the optimal value function.
elements.sent-2411-009: We extend $\Alg$ to handle non-linear FA and evaluate its empirical performance on the MuJoCo and Atari benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-010: Our results demonstrate that $\Alg$ consistently achieves similar or better performance compared to $\MDPO$, $\PPO$ and $\TRPO$.
elements.sent-2411-011: Policy gradient (PG) methods~\citep{williams1992simple,sutton1999policy,konda2000actor,kakade2001natural have been critical to the achievements of reinforcement learning (RL).}
elements.sent-2411-012: Although the PG objective is non-concave, recent theoretical research~\citep{agarwal2021theory,mei2020global,mei2021leveraging,bhandari2021linear,lan2023policy,shani2020adaptive,liu2024elementary,lu2024towards,alfano2022linear,yuan2023linear has analyzed PG methods in simplified settings and demonstrated their global convergence to an optimal policy.}
elements.sent-2411-013: While such simplified analyses are helpful in understanding the underlying optimization issues, the resulting methods are rarely used in practice.
elements.sent-2411-014: On the other hand, while methods such as TRPO~\citep{schulman2015trust , PPO~\citep{schulman2017proximal , MDPO~\citep{tomar2020mirror are commonly used in deep RL, their theoretical analysis in the function approximation setting is quite limited.}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: In particular, existing work either (i) analyzes these methods only in the impractical tabular setting~\citep{tomar2020mirror,shani2020adaptive or (ii) modifies these algorithms to make them more amenable to theoretical analysis~\citep{liu1906neural,zhong2024theoretical .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Unfortunately, these modified algorithms are quite different from the original variants and are not systematically benchmarked on standard environments.
elements.sent-2411-017: Consequently, there exists a large gap between PG methods that have theoretical guarantees in realistic settings versus those which are implemented in practice.
elements.sent-2411-018: To make matters worse, it has been demonstrated that code-level implementation details impact the empirical performance more than the underlying algorithm~\citep{engstrom2019implementation .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Designing theoretically principled PG algorithms that simultaneously have good empirical performance on the standard set of benchmarks is the main motivation behind this work.
elements.sent-2411-020: To that end, we leverage an algorithm first proposed by~\citet{vaswani2021general , which we modify to remove the need for normalization.}
elements.sent-2411-021: We coin this refinement Softmax Policy Mirror Ascent (referred to as $\Alg$).
elements.sent-2411-022: We show that $\Alg$ has comparable convergence guarantees as existing theoretical techniques~\citep{lu2024towards,yuan2023linear in the tabular and function approximation settings, while achieving comparable practical performance as PPO, TRPO and MDPO, without additional algorithmic modifications.}
elements.sent-2411-023: In particular, we make the following contributions.
elements.sent-2411-024: Contribution 1: In~\cref{sec:algorithm , we focus on the multi-armed bandit and tabular MDP settings, where the number of parameters scales with the number of states and actions.}
elements.sent-2411-025: We develop the $\Alg$ algorithm, which parameterizes the policy using the softmax function and uses a mirror ascent (with the log-sum-exp mirror map) update.
elements.sent-2411-026: Compared to $\NPG$ that can be viewed as mirror ascent in the space of probabilities, $\Alg$ corresponds to mirror ascent in the dual space of logits and does not require a normalization across actions.
elements.sent-2411-027: Given access to the exact policy gradients, we prove that $\Alg$ with a constant step-size converges to the optimal policy at a linear rate and thus matches the rate of $\NPG$~\citep{khodadadian2021linear,liu2024elementary .}
elements.sent-2411-028: In comparison, constant step-size softmax policy gradient ($\SPG$)~\citep{agarwal2021theory,mei2020global can only achieve sublinear convergence rates even with Nesterov acceleration~\citep{chenaccelerated .}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Hence, by changing the mirror map (from Euclidean to log-sum-exp) while using the same policy parameterization, $\Alg$ can result in an exponential improvement over $\SPG$.
elements.sent-2411-030: Contribution 2: In order to handle MDPs with large state-action spaces, we use function approximation (e.g. linear models or neural networks) to parameterize the policies resulting in the class of log-linear or energy-based policies~\citep{haarnoja2017reinforcement,agarwal2021theory,yuan2023linear respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-031: By interpreting the policy parameterization as a constraint on the corresponding logits, we use projected mirror ascent to extend $\Alg$ to the FA setting and design~\cref{alg:spma .}
elements.sent-2411-032: Unlike that for $\NPG$, generalizing $\Alg$ does not require compatible function approximation, and thus results in a more practical algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-033: Unlike $\MDPO$~\citep{tomar2020mirror which results in non-convex surrogates for linear FA, $\Alg$ requires solving convex softmax classification problems in each iteration.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Contribution 3: In~\cref{sec:fa-theory , we state the conditions under which~\cref{alg:spma converges to the neighbourhood of the optimal value function, and characterize the resulting linear convergence rate.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: Hence, for log-linear policies,~\cref{alg:spma matches the theoretical convergence of $\NPG$ with compatible function approximation~\citep{agarwal2021theory,alfano2022linear,yuan2023linear .}}
elements.sent-2411-036: Our theoretical results are better than those in~\citet{vaswani2021general and~\citet{schulman2015trust which prove sublinear convergence to a stationary point for idealized variants of $\Alg$ and TRPO respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: In contrast to~\citet{kuba2022mirror which prove that the idealized variants of $\PPO$ and $\TRPO$ converge to the optimal policy asymptotically, we characterize the non-asymptotic convergence rate for~\cref{alg:spma .}}
elements.sent-2411-038: Contribution 4: We empirically evaluate $\Alg$ across simple MDPs with tabular and linear parameterization, Atari games with a discrete action space and a neural policy parameterization with CNNs, and continuous control MuJoCo tasks with a continuous action space and a neural policy parameterization with MLPs.
elements.sent-2411-039: We demonstrate that $\Alg$ has consistently good performance -- on Atari games $\Alg$ achieves better results than both $\TRPO$ and $\PPO$ while matching or outperforming $\MDPO$, whereas on MuJoCo tasks, $\Alg$ outperforms $\PPO$ and achieves similar or better results than $\MDPO$.
elements.sent-2411-040: We consider an infinite-horizon discounted Markov decision process (MDP)~\citep{puterman2014markov defined by $\cM = \langle \cS , \cA, \cP, r, \rho, \gamma \rangle$, where $\cS$ and $\cA$ represent the states and actions, $\mathcal{P : \cS \times \cA \rightarrow \Delta_{\cS}$ is the transition probability function, $r: \cS \times \cA \rightarrow [0, 1]$ is the reward function, $\rho \in \Delta_{\cS}$ is the initial state distribution, and $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ represents the discount factor.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: In this paper, we exclusively consider the setting where the number of states and actions is finite, but potentially large.
elements.sent-2411-042: Given $s \in \cS$, the policy $\pi$ induces a probability distribution $\ppi(.
elements.sent-2411-043: The action-value function $\qpi: \cS \times \cA \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ induced by $\pi$ is defined as $\qpi(s, a) := \E[\sum_{t=0 ^{\infty \gamma^t r(s_t, a_t)| s_0= s , a_0 = a]$ where $s_t \sim p(.}}
elements.sent-2411-044: The value function corresponding to $\qpi$ starting from state $s$ is defined as $V^{\pi (s) := \E_{a \sim \ppi(.}}
elements.sent-2411-045: Furthermore, the advantage function $\api: \cS \times \cA \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is defined as $\api(s, a) := \qpi(s, a) - V^{\pi (s)$.}
elements.sent-2411-046: The policy also induces a discounted state-occupancy measure $\dpi(s) := (1 - \gamma) \sum_{t=0 ^{\infty \gamma^t \prpi[s_t = s | s_0 \sim \rho]$ over the states.}}
elements.sent-2411-047: The objective is to find an optimal policy $\pi^*$ that maximizes the expected reward $J(\pi) $, i.e. $\pi^* = \argmax_{\pi J(\pi)$.}
elements.sent-2411-048: As a special case, in the bandit setting, $|\cS| = 1$, $|\cA| = K$, $\gamma=0$, and $J(\pi) = \langle \pi , r\rangle$, with $K$ representing the number of arms.
elements.section-2411-003: Softmax Policy Mirror Ascent: Tabular Parameterization
elements.sent-2411-049: Softmax policy mirror ascent (referred to as $\Alg$) represents the policy using the softmax function $h: \R^{A \rightarrow \Delta_{A}$ i.e. $\pi(\cdot|s) = h(\z(s,\cdot))$ s.t.}
elements.sent-2411-050: We first focus on the tabular parameterization where the number of parameters scales with the number of states and actions, and aim to learn the logits corresponding to an optimal policy.
elements.sent-2411-051: With some abuse of notation, we use $J(\z)$ to refer to $J(\pi)$ where $\pi(\cdot|s) = h(z(s,\cdot))$ and state the objective as: $\max_{\z \in \R^{SA J(\z)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-052: As the name suggests, $\Alg$ uses mirror ascent (MA) to maximize $J(\z)$.
elements.sent-2411-054: Hence, the MA update at iteration $t$ can be interpreted as moving in the gradient direction $\nabla_{z J(\zt)$ while staying ``close'' to the logit $\zt$, where the proximity between logits is measured according to the Bregman divergence and weighted by $\frac{1 {\eta}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-055: It is instructive to first instantiate the $\Alg$ update for the bandit setting where $J(\pi) = \langle \pi, r \rangle$.
elements.sent-2411-057: Following~\citet{vaswani2021general , we use the log-sum-exp mirror map i.e. $\phi(z) = \ln(\sum_{a \exp(z(a))$.}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Since $[\nabla \phi(z)](a) = \frac{\exp(z(a)) {\sum_{a' \exp(z(a')) = [h(z)](a) = \pi(a)$, the logit and probability spaces are dual to each other, and the $\nabla \phi$ map can be used to move between these spaces.}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: Given this, the $\Alg$ update can be written as: \begin{equation}\ppitt(a) = \ppit(a) \, (1 + \eta \, [r(a) - \inner{\pi}{r}] ) \nonumber = \ppit(a) \; [1 + \eta \sum_{a' \neq a} \ppit(a') \, \Delta(a, a')] \,, \label{eq:tab-mab-spma}\end{equation} where $\Delta(a, a') := r(a) - r(a')$ represents the reward gap between arms $a$ and $a'$.
elements.sent-2411-060: We first ensure that $\ppitt$ is a valid probability distribution.
elements.sent-2411-061: Since $r(a) \in [0,1]$ for all $a$, $\eta \le 1$ is sufficient to guarantee that $\ppitt(a)$ is non-negative for every $a$.
elements.sent-2411-063: Hence, for $\eta \leq 1$,~\cref{eq:tab-mab-spma results in a valid update to the policy.}
elements.sent-2411-064: The above update is related to the PROD algorithm~\citep{cesa2007improved used for the experts problem in the online learning literature.}
elements.sent-2411-065: In contrast to $\Alg$ which is derived from mirror ascent, PROD is derived using a linearization of the Hedge~\citep{freund1997decision algorithm and requires explicit normalization to obtain probabilities.}
elements.sent-2411-066: In order to extend $\Alg$ to the MDP setting, we use a (state-wise) weighted log-sum-exp mirror map, i.e. for a logit $z \in \R^{SA}$, we define $\Phi(z):= \sum_{s w(s) \, \phi(z(s,\cdot)) = \sum_{s w(s) \ln(\sum_{a}\exp(z(s, a))$ where $w(s)$ are the per-state weights.}}
elements.sent-2411-067: Following the proof of~\citet[Lemma 11]{vaswani2024decision , the resulting Bregman divergence is given as: $D_\Phi(z,z') = \sum_{s w(s) \, \text{KL (\pi'(\cdot|s) || \pi(\cdot|s))$ where $\pi$ and $\pi'$ are the policies corresponding to logits $z$ and $z'$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: At iteration $t$ of $\Alg$, we choose $w(s) = d^{\ppit (s)$ and use the policy gradient theorem~\citep{sutton1999policy to calculate $[\nabla J(\zt)](s,a) = d^{\ppit (s) \, \ppit(a|s) \, \apit(s,a)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: The resulting $\Alg$ update is given as: \begin{equation}\ztt = \argmax_{\z \in \R^{SA}} \sum_{s} d^{\ppit}(s) \bigg[ \langle \ppit(\cdot|s) \, \apit(s,\cdot), \, z(s,\cdot) \rangle - \frac{1}{\eta} \, KL(\ppit(\cdot|s) \, || \, h(z(s,\cdot)) \bigg] \,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-070: Since the above maximization decomposes over the states, we can write the per-state update for each $s \in \cS$ in terms of $\ppitt(\cdot|s) = h(\ztt(s,\cdot))$ as follows: \begin{equation}\ppitt(a | s) = \ppit(a | s) \, (1 + \eta \apit(s, a)) \,. \label{eq:tab-mdp-spma}\end{equation} Similar to the bandit case, since $r(s, a) \in [0,1]$, $\ppitt(a|s)$ is non-negative for $\eta \le 1-\gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-071: Since $\sum_{a \ppit(a | s) \apit(s, a) = 0$, $\sum \ppitt(a|s) = 1$, and hence~\cref{eq:tab-mdp-spma results in a valid policy update.}}
elements.sent-2411-072: In order to compare the $\Alg$ update to existing methods, note that for the tabular parameterization, natural policy gradient (NPG) update~\citep{kakade2001natural is the same as policy mirror ascent~\citep{lan2023policy,johnson2023optimal,xiao2022convergence and is given by: $\ppitt(a|s) \propto \ppit(a|s) \, \exp(\eta \, \apit(s,a))$.}}
elements.sent-2411-073: In contrast to $\NPG$, the $\Alg$ update in~\cref{eq:tab-mdp-spma is linear in both $\eta$ and $\apit(s,a)$ and does not require an explicit normalization across actions to ensure valid probability distributions.}
elements.sent-2411-074: On the other hand, softmax policy gradient (SPG)~\citep{agarwal2021theory,mei2020global corresponds to choosing the mirror map $\phi$ in~\cref{eq:md-update to be the Euclidean norm and has the following update: $\ztt(s,a) = \zt(s,a) + \eta \, \ppit(a|s) \apit(s,a)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-075: Compared to SPG that uses the softmax policy gradient to update the logits, $\Alg$ uses the softmax policy gradient to directly update the probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-076: As we demonstrate in the next section, this desirable property enables $\Alg$ to achieve faster rates than SPG.

Interact with the environment using $\ppit$ and form the surrogate function $\ell_t(\theta)$ in~\cref{eq:approx-surrogate} 2. Initialize inner-loop: $\omega_0 = \theta_{t}$ \For{$k \leftarrow 0$ \KwTo $m-1$}{ $\omega_{k+1} = \omega_{k} - \zeta \, \nabla_{\omega} \ell_t(\omega_k)$ } 3. $\theta_{t+1} = \omega_{m}$ 4. Update $\ppitt(\cdot|s) = h(f_{\theta_{t+1}}(s,.))$ } Return $\theta_{T}$

elements.section-2411-011: Additional Details for Stable Baselines Experiments

elements.subsection-2411-013: Ablation Study on the Number of Inner Loop Optimization Steps
elements.sent-2411-252: In this subsection, we investigate the effect of varying the number of inner loop optimization steps ($m$) in the stable baselines experiments.
elements.sent-2411-253: Consistent with~\citet{tomar2020mirror , we observe that using $m=1$ results in poor performance, so we focus on larger values of $m$.}
elements.sent-2411-254: In the MuJoCo experiments, increasing $m$ from 5 to 10 and 15 consistently improves the performance of $\Alg$ (see~\cref{fig:mujoco-m-ablation ).}
elements.sent-2411-255: Specifically, for larger $m$, $\Alg$ becomes comparable to $\TRPOC$ on Hopper and Ant, while outperforming it on HalfCheetah (see~\cref{fig:mujoco-m-vertical ).}
elements.sent-2411-256: For the Atari experiments, we observe that increasing $m$ does not necessarily improve the results across methods (see~\cref{fig:atari-m-ablation ).}
elements.sent-2411-257: We conjecture that this is a side-effect of using a constant tuned step-size (for $m = 5$) in the inner-loop.
elements.sent-2411-258: In the future, we plan to run the full grid-search for the inner-loop step-size for each value of $m$.
elements.sent-2411-259: Alternatively, we plan to investigate an adaptive way of setting the inner-loop step-size.
